Lesson 1 What in the World?
animal
an·i·mal
noun
A living thing that needs food and water to live.

ex·tinct
adjective
When a species dies out forever, it is extinct.

en·dan·gered
adjective
An endangered species has very few animals left.

hab·i·tat

A habitat is where an animal lives in nature.

noun
The physical world including plants, animals, water, and air.

balanced
bal·anced
adjective
A balanced habitat has the right amount of each animal.

connected
con·nect·ed
adjective
Humans and animals are connected — we affect each other.

save
save

plant

tree
tree

air
air

survive
sur·vive
verb

pro·vide
verb

grow
grow
verb

Lesson 3 Plants
root
root
noun
Most plants have roots. The roots usually grow down, into the soil.
Translation: select a language above
common
com·mon
adjective
Some plants are common. They grow in many places.

rare
rare
adjective
Some plants are rare. They grow in only a few places and can be hard to find.

edible
ed·i·ble
adjective
Some plants are edible. They are safe to eat.

poisonous
poi·son·ous
adjective
Some plants are poisonous. They can make you sick.

flower
flow·er
noun
Many plants have flowers. These plants are called flowering plants.

identify
i·den·ti·fy
verb
You can identify a plant by looking at its flowers and other parts.

domestic
do·mes·tic
adjective
Humans feed domestic animals and give them a place to live.

wild
wild
adjective
Wild animals live in nature, find their own food, and make their own places to live.

amphibian
am·phib·i·an
noun
Amphibians are small animals that live on land and in water.

bird
bird
noun
A bird is an animal that has wings and is covered in feathers.

fish
fish
noun
A fish is an underwater animal that has fins and is covered with scales.

mammal
mam·mal
noun
A mammal is covered in fur or hair and produces milk for its young.

reptile
rep·tile
noun
A reptile has dry, scaly skin and lays eggs on land.

class
class
noun
An animal class is made up of animals that have similar features.

Lesson 5 Break It Down!
organism
or·gan·ism
noun
An organism is any living thing. You are an organism. A plant is an organism.
Translation: select a language above
insect
in·sect
noun
An insect is a small type of animal with six legs.
Translation: select a language above
worm
worm
noun
A worm is a small animal that does not have legs or a spine.
Translation: select a language above
fungus
fun·gus
noun
A fungus is a type of organism that often lives on dead or decaying things.
Translation: select a language above
bacteria
bac·te·ri·a
noun
Bacteria are very tiny organisms that are everywhere, but you need a microscope to see them.
Translation: select a language above
decomposer
de·com·pos·er
noun
A decomposer feeds on other organisms after they die. Insects, bacteria, fungi, and worms are decomposers.
Translation: select a language above
decay
de·cay
verb
Breaking apart a dead plant or a dead animal helps it decay and become part of the soil.
Translation: select a language above
nutrient
nu·tri·ent
noun
A nutrient is anything an organism eats to survive and grow.
Translation: select a language above
Lesson 6 Habitats
desert
des·ert
noun
A desert is a large area of land that gets little to no rain.
Translation: select a language above
forest
for·est
noun
A forest is a large area with many trees.
Translation: select a language above
grassland
grass·land
noun
A grassland is a flat area of land covered by grass.
Translation: select a language above
savanna
sa·van·na
noun
A savanna is a flat area covered by grass and scattered trees in tropical places.
Translation: select a language above
tundra
tun·dra
noun
A tundra is a very cold and windy area.
Translation: select a language above
freshwater
fresh·wa·ter
adjective
Freshwater habitats include lakes, streams, and rivers. The water is not salty.

marine
ma·rine
adjective
Marine habitats are found in the ocean, which is salty.
Translation: select a language above
coral reef
cor·al reef
noun
A coral reef is a type of marine habitat made up of rock-like living organisms called coral.

Lesson 7 Life Cycles
life cycle
life cy·cle
noun
A life cycle is the series of changes a living thing goes through during its lifetime.
Translation: select a language above
reproduce
re·pro·duce
verb
When organisms reproduce, they create new organisms.
Translation: select a language above
seed
seed
noun
Plants produce seeds to create new plants.
Translation: select a language above
begin
be·gin
verb
To begin is to start. The life cycle of a plant begins with a seed.
Translation: select a language above
end
end
verb
To end is to stop or be finished. The life cycle of a plant ends when the plant dies.
Translation: select a language above
develop
de·vel·op
verb
Plants can develop leaves and flowers.
Translation: select a language above
young
young
adjective
A young plant has lived for a short time.
Translation: select a language above
mature
ma·ture
adjective
A mature plant is fully grown or developed.
Translation: select a language above
Lesson 8 Energy and the Food Chain
energy
en·er·gy
noun
Living things need energy to grow and move.
Translation: select a language above
omnivore
om·ni·vore
noun
An omnivore eats plants and animals. A bear is an omnivore.
Translation: select a language above
carnivore
car·ni·vore
noun
A carnivore eats animals. A tiger is a carnivore.
Translation: select a language above
herbivore
her·bi·vore
noun
An herbivore eats plants. A panda is an herbivore.
Translation: select a language above
consumer
con·sum·er
noun
A consumer eats plants or animals. Omnivores, carnivores, and herbivores are consumers.
Translation: select a language above
producer
pro·duc·er
noun
A producer makes its own food. Plants are producers.
Translation: select a language above
predator
pred·a·tor
noun
A predator is an animal that hunts and eats other animals.
Translation: select a language above
prey
prey
noun
Prey is an animal that is hunted and eaten by a predator. Zebras are prey.
Translation: select a language above
Lesson 9 Natural Resources
natural
nat·u·ral
adjective
Something that is natural comes from nature, like wood from trees.
Translation: select a language above
resource
re·source
noun
A resource is something we use. Wood is a natural resource we use to build things.
Translation: select a language above
electricity
e·lec·tric·i·ty
noun
Electricity is a type of energy we use in our homes and at school.
Translation: select a language above
fossil fuel
fos·sil fu·el
noun
Oil, coal, and gas are fossil fuels. We burn fossil fuels to produce heat and electricity.
Translation: select a language above
solar
so·lar
adjective
Solar energy comes from sunlight. We use solar energy to produce electricity.
Translation: select a language above
wind
wind
noun
Wind is the air that blows around us. We use energy from wind to produce electricity.
Translation: select a language above
renewable
re·new·a·ble
adjective
A renewable resource never runs out. Solar energy from the sun is a renewable resource.
Translation: select a language above
nonrenewable
non·re·new·a·ble
adjective
A nonrenewable resource is limited. Coal is a nonrenewable resource that could run out.
Translation: select a language above

liquid
liq·uid
noun
The water you drink is a liquid.
Translation: select a language above
solid
sol·id
noun
Water in the form of ice is a solid.

gas
gas
noun
Water in the form of water vapor is a gas. Water vapor is also called steam.

atmosphere
at·mos·phere
noun
The atmosphere is the layer of gases that surrounds Earth.

cloud
cloud
noun
Each cloud is made up of water vapor, tiny bits of ice, or tiny drops of liquid water.

return
re·turn
verb
Water returns, or comes back, to Earth through rain.
Translation: select a language above
collect
col·lect
verb
You can collect rainwater in a bucket.
Translation: select a language above
become
be·come
verb
Water can become, or turn into, ice.

problem
prob·lem
noun
A problem is something that causes difficulty or worry.

solution
so·lu·tion
noun
A solution is something that makes something better.

damage
dam·age
verb
When we cut down trees, we might damage an ecosystem.

fix
fix
verb
Planting new trees can help fix the problem of deforestation.

waste
waste
verb
When we use something we do not need, we waste it.

conserve
con·serve
verb
When we turn off a faucet, we save or conserve water.

notice
no·tice
verb
When you look at trees, you might notice beautiful leaves and flowers.

ignore
ig·nore
verb
When you pay no attention to something, you ignore it.

Lesson 12 Protecting Our Planet
garbage
gar·bage
noun
Garbage is all the things we throw out.
Translation: select a language above
litter
lit·ter
noun
Litter is garbage that people throw on the ground.
Translation: select a language above
pollute
pol·lute
verb
Litter and toxic chemicals pollute the environment, making it less safe.
Translation: select a language above
toxic
tox·ic
adjective
Things that are toxic are not safe to eat, drink, touch, or breathe.
Translation: select a language above
chemical
chem·i·cal
noun
A chemical can be a liquid, solid, or gas. Some chemicals are toxic.
Translation: select a language above
release
re·lease
verb
Some factories release toxic chemicals as gases into the air.
Translation: select a language above
smog
smog
noun
Smog is air filled with toxic chemicals.
Translation: select a language above
contaminate
con·tam·i·nate
verb
Liquid chemicals can contaminate water and make it dangerous to drink.
Translation: select a language above
Lesson 13 Reduce, Reuse, Recycle
reduce
re·duce
verb
Turn off the lights when you leave a room to reduce how much electricity you use.
Translation: select a language above
reuse
re·use
verb
When you reuse something, you use it again instead of throwing it away.
Translation: select a language above
recycle
re·cy·cle
verb
Recycle plastic, paper, and metal items so they can be made into new items.
Translation: select a language above
bin
bin
noun
Recycle plastic, paper, and metal items by putting them in a recycling bin.
Translation: select a language above
protect
pro·tect
verb
Reducing, reusing, and recycling help protect the planet so it stays healthy.
Translation: select a language above
container
con·tain·er
noun
A container holds something. You can recycle many types of containers.
Translation: select a language above
empty
emp·ty
adjective
When something is empty, it has nothing in it. Only empty containers go in a recycling bin.
Translation: select a language above
biodegrade
bi·o·de·grade
verb
Biodegrade means to slowly decay over time and become part of the soil.
